records.author: Amichai Eliyahu
records.context: The “spirit of Oslo” refers to the peace-process logic associated with the Oslo Accords: negotiation, compromise, mutual recognition, and the pursuit of a political settlement with the Palestinian side.
records.statement: “The spirit of Oslo is still here. And if we don't get rid of it … we will lose again. With Nazis & their assistants, you don't talk. You eradicate evil from the world. Do you want your pop. [in Gaza] to drink? Release the hostages. Now”
records.homogenization_anchor: With Nazis & their assistants, you don't talk
records.immutability_anchor: With Nazis & their assistants, you don't talk
records.essentialization_anchor:
You eradicate evil from the world.;
With Nazis & their assistants, you don't talk
records.immutability_bj: As an othering strategy, the statement suggests immutability by saying “you don’t talk” with them and must “eradicate evil,” implying that the enemy cannot be changed or negotiated with.
records.essentialization_bj: As an othering strategy, the statement essentializes the enemy by calling them “Nazis” and “evil,” making evil appear as their defining characteristic.
records.retaliation_anchor: Do you want your pop. [in Gaza] to drink? Release the hostages. Now
records.retaliation_bj: As a just-cause strategy, the statement makes withholding water appear justified as pressure for the release of hostages.
records.meaning_anchor: You eradicate evil from the world.
records.meaning_bj: As an individual-needs strategy, the statement gives the action a larger moral purpose by framing it as the need to “eradicate evil from the world,” turning the conflict into a morally necessary struggle beyond ordinary political negotiation.
records.no_alternative_framing_anchor: The spirit of Oslo is still here. And if we don't get rid of it … we will lose again
records.no_alternative_framing_bj: As a rhetorical-foreclosure strategy, the statement excludes negotiation and compromise as legitimate options by portraying the “spirit of Oslo” as a path to defeat and claiming that “with Nazis & their assistants, you don’t talk,” but must “eradicate evil from the world.”

records.meaning_anchor: Muslim land in the Land of Israel; Gaza should be left as a monument, like Sodom

records.author: Ariel Kallner
records.statement: “Nakba to the enemy now! This day is our Pearl Harbor. We will still learn the lessons. Right now, one goal: Nakba! A Nakba that will overshadow the Nakba of 48. A Nakba in Gaza and a Nakba for anyone who dares to join!…”
records.retaliation_anchor: This day is our Pearl Harbor.
records.retaliation_bj: Retaliation works as a just-cause strategy by framing the day as “our Pearl Harbor,” emotionalizing the prior attack as a collective trauma; this legitimizes a “Nakba in Gaza” and against anyone who joins as a justified response.
records.meaning_bj: As an individual-needs strategy, the statement gives the demanded “Nakba” a larger historical meaning by framing it as a historically significant act of retaliation, one that is meant to overshadow the Nakba of 1948 and mark an unprecedented rupture after decades of conflict.

records.author: Bezalel Smotrich
records.context: This reflects Smotrich's far-right political stance, which advocates for Jewish settlement in the West Bank and denies the legitimacy of Palestinian claims to the land.
records.statement: "The 'settler violence' campaign is an anti-Semitic lie spread by the enemies of Israel with the aim of discrediting the pioneer settlers and the settlement enterprise and harming them, and through them discrediting the entire State of Israel. This is an anti-moral BDS campaign that turns the victim into the aggressor and permits the blood of the settlers. It is a shame that a government in their hands I cooperate with this in the days when the settlers pay a dear price in blood in the war in Gaza. With the help of God, I will continue to act fearlessly to strengthen and develop Jewish settlement in all parts of the Land of Israel and to strive for sustainable peace, which will only be achieved when the hope of the Arabs to establish an Arab state on the ruins of the Jewish state is dashed If the price is the imposition of American sanctions on me - so be it."

 If the price is the imposition of American sanctions on me - so be it.

records.author: Bezalel Smotrich
records.context: Amalek is a biblical tribe hostile to the Israelites, described in the bible as the enemy of the Israelites. The relationship between the two groups is mainly hostile. The story of Amalek serves as a symbol of evil and enmity against the people of God in Jewish tradition, and the command to remember what Amalek did to the Israelites and the obligation to blot out their memory is commemorated annually in the Jewish tradition during the Sabbath before Purim.
records.statement: “Moments before redemption, we must not hesitate. We must destroy Rafah, Nusseirat, & Dir al-Balah 'wipe out the memory of Amalek! …There's no half- measure. Rafah, Dir al-Balah Nusseirat absolute destruction!”

records.essentialization_bj: As an othering strategy, the statement essentializes the targeted population by invoking “Amalek,” portraying them as an inherently evil enemy whose destruction appears necessary.
records.meaning_anchor: Wipe out the memory of Amalek

Amalek is a  biblical tribe hostile to the Israelites, described in the bible as the enemy of the Israelites. The relationship between the two groups is mainly hostile. The story of Amalek serves as a symbol of evil and enmity against the people of God in Jewish tradition, and the command to remember what Amalek did to the Israelites and the obligation to blot out their memory is commemorated annually in the Jewish tradition during the Sabbath before Purim.
records.statement: “Every Jew knows the saying 'Remember what Amalek did unto thee by the way […]' and that is what [Hamas] did and their judgement shall be to destroy them, full stop. I relate to them like Amalek."
records.immutability_anchor: what Amalek did unto thee by the way […]' and that is what [Hamas] did and their judgement shall be to destroy them, full stop
records.essentialization_anchor: I relate to them like Amalek
records.immutability_bj: As an enemy-image strategy, the statement invokes the biblical figure of Amalek to portray Hamas as an enemy for whom negotiation or transformation is not imaginable, only destruction as a predetermined judgement.
records.essentialization_bj: As an enemy-image strategy, the statement essentializes Hamas by comparing them to Amalek, a symbolic enemy figure associated with absolute evil and destruction, thereby portraying Hamas as an inherently evil enemy.
records.retaliation_anchor: Remember what Amalek did unto thee by the way […]' and that is what [Hamas] did
records.retaliation_bj: The Amalek quotation is used to identify Hamas’s act with an enemy whose judgment is ‘to destroy them,’ legitimating destruction as the response to what Hamas did.
records.meaning_anchor: Remember what Amalek did unto thee; their judgement shall be to destroy them; I relate to them like Amalek
records.meaning_bj: As an individual-needs strategy, the statement gives the destruction of Hamas a larger religious-historical meaning by comparing Hamas to Amalek and framing their destruction as a fixed moral judgement within a biblical enemy narrative.
